title: Tiling an external step with quarry tiles
subtitle: Lay frost-resistant tiles on a concrete doorstep with a draining fall
excerpt: How to tile an external concrete doorstep or entrance step with quarry tiles. Covers checking the existing concrete, setting out with a draining fall, applying flexible adhesive, grouting with a waterproof grout, and the safety finish.
sourceNotes: BS 5385-4: Wall and floor tiling; code of practice for tiling and mosaics in specific conditions (external). British Ceramic Research Association (now CERAM) external tiling guidance. City and Guilds Level 2 Tiling. Collins Complete DIY Manual (1989).

glossaryTerms.definition: An unglazed ceramic tile, originally made from natural clay or shale, fired at high temperature. Dense, frost-resistant, and slip-resistant when dry. Standard sizes are 150 x 150 mm and 150 x 300 mm, in thicknesses of 9 to 12 mm. The unglazed surface weathers and ages well outdoors without losing its texture.

A deliberate slope built into a tiled surface to direct water away from a building or doorway, preventing ponding. The standard fall for external paving is 1:60 (approximately 1.7 mm per 100 mm of run) — enough to drain efficiently without feeling sloped underfoot.

A polymer-modified cementitious adhesive that retains a degree of flexibility after curing. Required for external and floor tiling where thermal expansion and substrate movement would crack a standard rigid adhesive. Classified to EN 12004 as C2 (improved cementitious adhesive) or C2F (fast-setting version).

content.text: An external step needs tiles that can withstand frost, wet, and foot traffic.
are the right choice: dense, unglazed, and frost-resistant. The job is more demanding than indoor tiling because the substrate must be checked for movement, the tiles must be laid with a
away from the door, and the adhesive must be
to cope with thermal movement. A ridge or hollow in the tiled surface that traps water will crack in the first frost.

Quarry tiles (frost-rated, calculate area plus 10% for cuts). Flexible C2 tile adhesive. Waterproof external grout. Tile spacers (3 mm is standard for quarry tiles). A notched trowel (6 mm V-notch for smaller tiles, 10 mm square-notch for thicker tiles). A grout float. A tile cutter or angle grinder with a diamond blade for cuts. A bucket and sponge. A spirit level. A tape measure. Safety goggles and gloves for cutting.

The existing concrete must be sound, not cracked or crumbling, and free of movement. Tap across the step: any hollow sound means the concrete is delaminating and must be repaired or replaced before tiling. Remove loose concrete, laitance, and any old paint with the SDS drill fitted with a chisel bit. Fill cracks and hollow areas with a rapid-setting concrete repair mortar and allow it to cure fully; at least 24 hours.
Step 2, set out with a drainage fall

The finished tile surface must slope away from the door threshold at a fall of 1 in 60. On a 600 mm deep step, this is a fall of 10 mm from back to front. Set out with the spirit level and a timber wedge to check the existing step fall; if it already slopes away from the building, your adhesive bed will be roughly even. If the step is flat or slopes inward, you will need to build up the adhesive bed at the front edge to create the required fall.

Dry-lay the tiles on the step with spacers in place to check the layout before any adhesive is mixed. Start from the back of the step and work toward the front edge, so any cut tiles fall at the front edge below the sightline. Mark the tile positions on the step in pencil. Remove the tiles.
Step 4, apply adhesive and lay tiles

Mix the flexible adhesive to a stiff, butter consistency. Apply a bed to the step with the flat face of the trowel, then comb with the notched trowel in one direction to leave an even ribbed surface. Back-butter each tile as well; quarry tiles are thick and porous enough to absorb moisture from a single bed before the adhesive can bond. Press each tile firmly into place with a slight twist to collapse the ribs, insert spacers, and check level and fall with the spirit level as you go. Wipe off any adhesive squeeze-out between tiles immediately. Allow the adhesive to cure as per the manufacturer's data sheet before grouting; typically 24 hours.

Use a waterproof or polymer-modified grout rated for external use. Mix to a stiff paste, spread diagonally across the tiles with a grout float, and push the grout firmly into the joints. Remove the spacers before the adhesive sets if they are not the drop-in type. Wipe off excess with a damp sponge, working diagonally to avoid dragging grout out of the joints. Allow the grout to firm, then polish off the haze with a dry cloth. Seal the finished surface with a silicone-based tile sealer after the grout has dried for 7 days.

attrs.cause: Insufficient back-buttering; adhesive did not achieve full coverage on the tile back.
attrs.fix: Lift the hollow tiles, clean the back and the substrate, back-butter generously, and re-lay. Hollow tiles on external surfaces will crack in frost.

attrs.symptom: Water pooling at the back of the step against the door threshold
attrs.cause: Drainage fall is insufficient or slopes in the wrong direction.
attrs.fix: Check the fall with a spirit level and a straightedge before laying. If the concrete substrate is sloping inward, build up the front edge of the adhesive bed to create the correct 1:60 fall.
